The resurrection is real enough to restore men to their work, yet strange enough to make them unrecognizable in the doing. Seven disciples return to fishing after the crucifixion—not out of hardened unbelief, but because they live in the gap between resurrection and return, suspended between what they know and what they cannot yet fully grasp. Their empty nets speak to the sufficiency of human effort alone, the futility of labor severed from the Master's presence and direction. But Christ does not condemn their return to the familiar. Instead, He meets them where they are, on the shoreline at dawn, and redirects their hands and their hearts with a command that transforms everything. One word from the risen Jesus—"Cast the net on the right side"—yields a catch so abundant the disciples cannot contain it. They recognize Him not through His face or His words, but through His power, through the weight of blessing that only He can give. And in that moment of recognition, Peter abandons caution and plunges into the sea, reckless with love, swimming toward the One who had become his consuming desire.

The breakfast scene that follows is intimate in a way that should arrest us. The risen Son of God, having conquered death and secured the redemption of the world, stands at a charcoal fire preparing a simple meal for His disciples. He serves them bread and fish with His own hands. There is no distance here, no ethereal apparition far removed from human need and comfort. He feeds them. He sits with them. And in this ordinariness—this radical availability of the God-man to His struggling followers—we encounter the heart of what the resurrection means: not escape from the created world, but its restoration and redemption. Christ is not leaving them as orphans. He is meeting them in their hunger, their confusion, their need.

Then comes the reckoning. Three times Jesus asks Peter if he loves Him, and three times Peter affirms it, his pain mounting with each repetition. The questions mirror Peter's three denials, but they move toward restoration rather than condemnation. With each affirmation, Jesus assigns Peter a commission: feed, shepherd, feed—the responsibility to tend the flock of God. Yet immediately after entrusting the Church to Peter's care, Jesus tells him plainly that this shepherd will not choose his own path. One day, old and helpless, Peter will be led where he does not want to go. He will stretch out his hands in death. The cost of loving Jesus, of shepherd the sheep, is written into his future as suffering. Christ does not soften this blow with false comfort. He simply says: "Follow Me." There is an authority and finality in those words that demands Peter choose suffering with Jesus over any other path.

When Peter immediately asks about another disciple—"Lord, what about him?"—Jesus cuts through the deflection with sharp clarity: "What is that to you? You follow Me." This is the word for every disciple in every age. Your call is not to compare your burden with another's, not to count their grace or measure their trial against your own. Your sole business is to follow. The risen Christ does not offer us the luxury of looking sideways at another's journey. He calls us forward into our own, into obedience that is personal and particular and costly. Everything else is distraction from the one thing that matters: that you follow Him.
